text:
$LITE Transcript from Conference Call: 

"The thing that keeps me up at night most is: Substrates. 

Less so sort of reactors and getting the right tools into the fab, although it’s not a zero challenge."

They basically went out and said $AXTI is the bottleneck for photonics. 

Especially if InP substrates keeps $LITE's CEO up at night. 

I've been mentioning it for the past few months, if AXT they control the substrate supply, they have a lot of pricing power over $LITE to $COHR and America's AI buildout. 

I've seen a ton of misinterpretation on X saying: "Seven-year agreement with Sumitomo". But they literally said there was supply tightness Today. And the agreement doesn't mean there will be enough supply for 7 years, especially when China export controls start to kick in on upstream feedstock/materials required by Japanese supply chains to make then. 

This is before Photonics has even started to ramp up.

My $AXTI bottleneck thesis is playing out real time and we got new confirmation from $LITE that InP substrates is a bottleneck.

My $AXTI bottleneck thesis is playing out real time and we got new confirmation from $LITE that InP substrates is a bottleneck."}}}, "previous_counts": {"bookmark_count": 3, "favorite_count": 7, "quote_count": 0, "reply_count": 0, "retweet_count": 0}, "rest_id": "2034932278032564264", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "78786", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
Yep agreed. Reason why memory/photonics is holding up against broader macro selloffs is that their net income (for memory) and revenue growth (for optical) are just way too high.   

If there were no Iran war $MU would likely be tons higher, but the core point still stands that most repricing happens before earnings, not on the actual day, unless there's a genuine surprise.

text:
Picks and shovels plays of the photonics boom

$LITE | Lumentum: lasers for silicon photonics transceivers and CPO

$TSEM | Tower Semiconductor: silicon photonics foundry

$BESI | BE Semiconductor: hybrid bonding and packaging equipment for photonics

$VECO | Veeco: manufacturing equipment for photonic and optical chips

$AIXA | Aixtron: tools used to grow photonic materials like GaAs and InP

$SIVE | Sivers Semiconductors: laser chips and arrays for optical links and silicon photonics

$AXTI | AXT: InP, GaAs, and Ge wafers for optical devices

$COHR | Coherent: lasers, photodiodes, and other optical parts for communications

$AAOI | Applied Optoelectronics: makes lasers and optical transceivers for datacenters

$6777 | Santec: tunable lasers and optical testing tools

$SMTC | Semtech: chips that help high-speed optical links work properly

$IQE | IQE: epitaxial wafers and materials for photonic chips

$POET | POET Technologies: platform for building optical engines in a more scalable, chip-like way

$ALMU | Aeluma: III-V devices on silicon, including laser-related tech for silicon photonics

$FN | Fabrinet: manufacturing and packaging for optical components

$MTSI | MACOM: photodiodes and other optical chips for telecom and data centers

$5802 | Sumitomo Electric: InP and GaAs wafers for lasers and optical communication devices

$6965 | Hamamatsu Photonics: photodiodes, optical sensors, and measurement components

$6754 | Anritsu: testing gear for fiber and high-speed optical networks

$6146 | DISCO: machines that cut, grind, and polish semiconductor wafers

text:
This $2B photonics monopoly down 75% from ATHs is key to AI's next paradigm shift.

Soitec at €54.00 $SOI.PA / $SLOIF is the clearest photonics multi-bagger I see right now.

After spending 36 hours at Nvidia GTC studying networking & CPO...

Soitec is now my third largest asymmetrical bet, behind $AAOI and $KRKNF.

Here's why I have steel conviction.

Soitec owns 95%+ market share in Photonics-SOI wafer, the substrate underneath every silicon photonics chip.

Every transceiver from $AAOI and $COHR. Every CPO switch from Nvidia, Broadcom, and Marvell. All fabricated on Soitec substrates.

Their only licensed competitor, GlobalWafers, is exiting.

There's an incredible moat here. No one else is qualified at all the foundries and it would take competitors years to even try and insert at $TSM $TSEM $GFS as they ramp for SiPho.

Why it's asymmetrical beyond the market growth: co-packaged optics requires 4x the SiPho wafer content vs. pluggable transceivers. 

As the industry migrates from pluggables to co-packaged optics, which Nvidia's SVP of Networking told us at GTC is not optional, Soitec's revenue per optical port quadruples.

That is asymmetry right there. 

Soitec's Edge & Cloud AI division which includes data center, is already growing 34% yoy, so when the CPO inflection happens in 27-28, we're talking silly math on top of existing momentum.

$TSEM their largest foundry customer, just reported silicon photonics revenue up 115% YoY with $920M in photonics capex committed and 70%+ of new capacity pre-reserved through 2028. Stock is up 60% and it's now a $20B company, but 10-15% of their cost per sipho chip will flow to Soitec.

The market primarily sees this as a struggling mobile supplier at 3.6x EV/revenue with the CEO stepping down. But the business has reached the trough of mobile and even has some nice short-term tailwinds in edge AI like smart glasses. 

I see a monopoly substrate provider sitting at the base of a $14B --> $73B optical interconnect TAM growing at 39% CAGR.

Full breakdown of my thesis on Substack below.

text:
The SOI Wafer You Didn't Know About — And the Patent War It Started

Not many people know that SOI wafers aren't just for RF chips. They're also the backbone of silicon photonics — the technology wiring up AI datacenters right now.

But here's a story most people outside Korea have never heard.
In the late 1990s, UC Berkeley's Chenming Hu invented the FinFET on SOI wafers. Problem: SOI is expensive, suffers from floating body effects, poor thermal conductivity, and increased leakage. Fine for research, not for mass production.

A Korean professor named Jong-Ho Lee (이종호) solved this. Working at Wonkwang University with KAIST, he built the FinFET on bulk silicon instead — fins connected directly to the substrate. Thermal and electrical problems solved. Cheap wafers. Manufacturable at scale.
He filed the U.S. patent on February 4, 2003. Intel filed theirs ten days later. Samsung and TSMC didn't file until 2004.
Intel paid ~$10M in licensing fees in 2012. Samsung refused — despite having collaborated with Prof. Lee and even calling him back to lecture their engineers before mass production. In 2016, KAIST's subsidiary sued Samsung in the Eastern District of Texas. Samsung tried everything: reporting Prof. Lee for unauthorized tech export, filing for patent invalidation (denied), pressuring his former university to claim ownership. None of it worked.
2018: U.S. jury awarded $400M. 2020: court judgment at $200M. Samsung settled for an undisclosed amount.

The twist? In 2022, Prof. Lee was appointed South Korea's Minister of Science and ICT. His declared assets: over $13M in deposits — "mostly from patent income."
Today every advanced logic chip from 22nm to 3nm uses bulk FinFET. The technology that made SOI unnecessary for logic was born from understanding exactly why SOI didn't work.
And SOI? It found a second life in silicon photonics.
Physics doesn't lie. 

And neither do patent filing dates.

text: After digging deeper, I found that Santec’s (https://t.co/UdZI8z5pVw) tunable laser was part of Advantest’s photonics test platform demo shown at SWTest in June 2024. Advantest (https://t.co/Cbc19Y9Alo) is yet to announce an optical instrument partner for its V93000-Triton photonics tester. By contrast, Teradyne’s ($TER) Photon 100 platform will likely integrate Quantifi’s optical instruments in a standard configuration (Teradyne acquired Quantifi in March 2025). But Teradyne also allows its customers to select their own manufacturing partners for high-volume production. Either way, Santec better be cooking something special.
embedded_text: Santec (https://t.co/Ydmx76C4PF) is upgrading its optical instruments for high-volume manufacturing. The TSL-580 tunable laser and SPA-200 photonics analyzer are designed for the top rack of a photonics test platform like this. Today, these types of instruments are primarily used https://t.co/MJnO0pXzf0

I just spot these before others do, and post information synthesis/discovery.

Ever stop to think… maybe the thesis/idea was right after all?

text:
💰 $NVDA continues investing in optical interconnects.

As GPU clusters scale beyond 100k units, electrical networking becomes a bottleneck. Photonics may be the only way to move data fast enough for next-gen AI models.

Infrastructure follows demand. #photonics #AI

Santec (https://t.co/UdZI8z5pVw) is upgrading its optical instruments for high-volume manufacturing. The TSL-580 tunable laser and SPA-200 photonics analyzer are designed for the top rack of a photonics test platform like this. Today, these types of instruments are primarily used in R&D, which is a much smaller market.

text:
I wouldn't say it's speculative. Hyperscalers have orders completely booked until 2028 from $LITE to $COHR.

Even $TSEM has 70% of their capacity booked and it's low fwd ~20 P/E 2028. 

CW/CPO more speculative for photonics, not the pluggable optical transceivers cycle. But compared to memory/GPUs, makes snse. 

But think everyones looking at photonics bottlenecks right low like pcb, which is funny

"text": "As of April 02, 2026
The AI Photonics and Optical Infrastructure sector is showing strong bullish momentum, led by a massive +20.34% surge in $AAOI. Applied Optoelectronics continues to ride a wave of back-to-back hyperscale order wins: a $200M+ 1.6T transceiver deal (believed to be Oracle https://t.co/plfJW8bp3d), a follow-on $53M 800G order for GPU cluster buildouts, and Rosenblatt's $140 price target reaffirmation citing long-term 10-15% market share potential and $4B revenue by 2027. Production ramp in Taiwan and Texas targeting 500K units/month by year-end adds supply-side conviction. (https://t.co/aPZ4muMf7y)
The broader 800G-to-1.6T transceiver migration cycle is pulling capital across the entire photonics value chain today. $AEHR is ripping on a new silicon photonics customer win for its FOX-XP burn-in systems, validating the picks-and-shovels trade into optical interconnect production. Compound semiconductor substrate names like $AXTI and $LWLG are catching a sympathy bid. The laggards at the bottom of the board, $SOI, $IQE, $SIVE, read more like OTC liquidity friction and pre-revenue dilution overhang than any fundamental deterioration in the optical super-cycle thesis.

$AAOI: +20.34%
$AEHR: +12.23%
$AXTI: +12.09%
$LWLG: +11.99%
$POET: +9.11%
$LITE: +8.14%

$BESIY: +2.62%

$MRVL: +0.37%

$SPX: +0.11%
$SIVEF: -0.71%
$GFS: -1.11%
$FPLSF: -3.29%
$SOI: -5.80%
$IQE: -5.98%
$SIVE: -11.21%

1. Optical Transceivers and Active Networking
AAOI, LITE, CIEN, COHR, FN: This is the epicenter of today's move. AAOI is the runaway leader on stacked hyperscale orders totaling $250M+ across 800G and 1.6T, with Rosenblatt slapping a $140 PT and Top Pick status. LITE and COHR ride the read-through as the transceiver migration thesis gets fresh order-book validation. CIEN benefits from the same AI networking bandwidth pull-through into DCI. FN stays steady as a connector/optical packaging play levered to the same volume ramp.
GLW: Corning is the fiber backbone play. Less explosive than pure transceiver names but 800G/1.6T volume means more fiber, more connectors, more infrastructure glass. Steady compounder in this tape.

2. Compound Semiconductor Substrates and Foundries
AXTI, TSEM, GFS: AXTI ripping +12% is the InP substrate read-through trade, pure and simple. Every AAOI transceiver order means more InP wafer demand, and AXTI is the US-listed proxy for that exposure. TSEM catches a bid as specialty foundry capacity tightens alongside transceiver production ramps. GFS lagging at -1.11% is likely macro/tariff noise weighing on the larger-cap foundry names.
IQE: Down nearly 6%. OTC liquidity dynamics and lingering dilution concerns continue to weigh on IQE despite its InP/GaAs exposure being thematically identical to AXTI. Brokerage friction on the London ordinary shares keeps this one in the penalty box.

3. Early-Stage Photonic Materials and PICs
POET, LWLG, ALMU, SIVEF, SIVE, FPLSF: POET +9% on continued momentum from its LITEON co-packaged optics partnership and Lessengers 1.6T collaboration. LWLG ripping nearly 12% as the EO polymer play catches a sympathy bid from the transceiver order flow headlines. ALMU steady at +4.8%. The ugly end of the board lives here too: SIVE cratering -11.21% and SIVEF -0.71% look like classic pre-revenue dilution overhang pressure. FPLSF down -3.29%, same OTC liquidity story.
BESIY: II-VI/Freiberger parent Besi holding +2.62%, quiet but consistent. Die-attach and packaging equipment demand tied to photonic IC assembly remains a slow-burn secular tailwind.

4. Semiconductor Equipment, Test, and Infrastructure
AEHR, FORM, EQUIP, KEYS, MRVL: AEHR is the standout at +12.23%. New silicon photonics customer win for FOX-XP wafer-level burn-in systems aimed at data center optical interconnect production. This is the picks-and-shovels validation trade into SiPho manufacturing scale-up. FORM +2.23% and EQUIP +1.78% grinding higher on general semi equipment sentiment. KEYS barely moving at +0.48%, not surprising given its diversified test portfolio dilutes the photonics-specific read-through.
MRVL: +0.37%, basically flat. Marvell is the large-cap anchor in the portfolio and moves on its own earnings/guidance cycle rather than single-day transceiver order catalysts. The DSP/PAM4 silicon story is intact but it takes more than one AAOI press release to move a $70B+ market cap.
SOI: Down -5.80%. Soitec continues to trade heavy on European semi demand concerns and SOI substrate inventory digestion. "text": "As of April 02, 2026
The AI Photonics and Optical Infrastructure sector is showing strong bullish momentum, led by a massive +20.34% surge in $AAOI. Applied Optoelectronics continues to ride a wave of back-to-back hyperscale order wins: a $200M+ 1.6T transceiver deal (believed to be Oracle https://t.co/plfJW8bp3d), a follow-on $53M 800G order for GPU cluster buildouts, and Rosenblatt's $140 price target reaffirmation citing long-term 10-15% market share potential and $4B revenue by 2027. Production ramp in Taiwan and Texas targeting 500K units/month by year-end adds supply-side conviction. (https://t.co/aPZ4muMf7y)
The broader 800G-to-1.6T transceiver migration cycle is pulling capital across the entire photonics value chain today. $AEHR is ripping on a new silicon photonics customer win for its FOX-XP burn-in systems, validating the picks-and-shovels trade into optical interconnect production. Compound semiconductor substrate names like $AXTI and $LWLG are catching a sympathy bid. The laggards at the bottom of the board, $SOI, $IQE, $SIVE, read more like OTC liquidity friction and pre-revenue dilution overhang than any fundamental deterioration in the optical super-cycle thesis.

$AAOI: +20.34%
$AEHR: +12.23%
$AXTI: +12.09%
$LWLG: +11.99%
$POET: +9.11%
$LITE: +8.14%
$CIEN: +7.79%
$TSEM: +5.74%
$ALMU: +4.82%
$FN: +4.30%
$COHR: +4.18%
$GLW: +3.89%
$BESIY: +2.62%
$FORM: +2.23%
$EQUIP: +1.78%
$KEYS: +0.48%
$MRVL: +0.37%
$NDX: +0.11%
$SPX: +0.11%
$SIVEF: -0.71%
$GFS: -1.11%
$FPLSF: -3.29%
$SOI: -5.80%
$IQE: -5.98%
$SIVE: -11.21%

1. Optical Transceivers and Active Networking
AAOI, LITE, CIEN, COHR, FN: This is the epicenter of today's move. AAOI is the runaway leader on stacked hyperscale orders totaling $250M+ across 800G and 1.6T, with Rosenblatt slapping a $140 PT and Top Pick status. LITE and COHR ride the read-through as the transceiver migration thesis gets fresh order-book validation. CIEN benefits from the same AI networking bandwidth pull-through into DCI. FN stays steady as a connector/optical packaging play levered to the same volume ramp.
GLW: Corning is the fiber backbone play. Less explosive than pure transceiver names but 800G/1.6T volume means more fiber, more connectors, more infrastructure glass. Steady compounder in this tape.

2. Compound Semiconductor Substrates and Foundries
AXTI, TSEM, GFS: AXTI ripping +12% is the InP substrate read-through trade, pure and simple. Every AAOI transceiver order means more InP wafer demand, and AXTI is the US-listed proxy for that exposure. TSEM catches a bid as specialty foundry capacity tightens alongside transceiver production ramps. GFS lagging at -1.11% is likely macro/tariff noise weighing on the larger-cap foundry names.
IQE: Down nearly 6%. OTC liquidity dynamics and lingering dilution concerns continue to weigh on IQE despite its InP/GaAs exposure being thematically identical to AXTI. Brokerage friction on the London ordinary shares keeps this one in the penalty box.

3. Early-Stage Photonic Materials and PICs
POET, LWLG, ALMU, SIVEF, SIVE, FPLSF: POET +9% on continued momentum from its LITEON co-packaged optics partnership and Lessengers 1.6T collaboration. LWLG ripping nearly 12% as the EO polymer play catches a sympathy bid from the transceiver order flow headlines. ALMU steady at +4.8%. The ugly end of the board lives here too: SIVE cratering -11.21% and SIVEF -0.71% look like classic pre-revenue dilution overhang pressure. FPLSF down -3.29%, same OTC liquidity story.
BESIY: II-VI/Freiberger parent Besi holding +2.62%, quiet but consistent. Die-attach and packaging equipment demand tied to photonic IC assembly remains a slow-burn secular tailwind.

4. Semiconductor Equipment, Test, and Infrastructure
AEHR, FORM, EQUIP, KEYS, MRVL: AEHR is the standout at +12.23%. New silicon photonics customer win for FOX-XP wafer-level burn-in systems aimed at data center optical interconnect production. This is the picks-and-shovels validation trade into SiPho manufacturing scale-up. FORM +2.23% and EQUIP +1.78% grinding higher on general semi equipment sentiment. KEYS barely moving at +0.48%, not surprising given its diversified test portfolio dilutes the photonics-specific read-through.
MRVL: +0.37%, basically flat. Marvell is the large-cap anchor in the portfolio and moves on its own earnings/guidance cycle rather than single-day transceiver order catalysts. The DSP/PAM4 silicon story is intact but it takes more than one AAOI press release to move a $70B+ market cap.
SOI: Down -5.80%. Soitec continues to trade heavy on European semi demand concerns and SOI substrate inventory digestion. The AI Photonics and Optical Infrastructure sector is posting broad-based gains today, led by a sharp surge in $LITE. Lumentum is riding a confluence of catalysts: Nvidia's $2B strategic investment cem its role in AI optical connectivity, plans to retrofit a 240,000-square-foot facility in Greensboro, NC for expanded InP laser production, and multiple price target hikes from BNP Paribas and Craig Hallum both north of $900. Zacks named LITE its Bull of the Day on AI optics momentum, Finviz piling additional tailwind on top of the Nvidia anchor. (https://t.co/Vreo0QOsO2)
Nvidia's parallel $2B bet on Marvell further validates the AI optics trade across the board, and the read-through is working. Nearly every name on the watchlist is green. Post-OFC 2026 hyperscaler capex conviction is holding. The optical super-cycle narrative is gaining institutional legs. The lone exception is $AXTI, which is bleeding out after B. Riley flagged troubling InP supply chain signals, including a key supplier denying bottlenecks while Sumitomo locked in a seven-year deal with a competitor, turning more cautious on the stock.

$MRVL: +7.73%
$CIEN: +7.00%

1. Optical Transceivers and Active Networking
LITE, COHR: Both are now S&P 500 constituents with Nvidia capital behind them. Today's move is not noise; it's re-rating. The 800G-to-1.6T migration has found its anchor suppliers and the street is pricing it accordingly.
CIEN, MRVL: Ciena's +7% confirms the read-through from hyperscaler network buildout. Marvell is getting the Nvidia halo treatment directly, with a fresh $2B partnership validating its AI networking silicon stack.
AAOI, FN, BESIY, FPLSF: The second-tier transceiver names are catching the coattails. AAOI and FN are moving on sector beta. BESIY and FPLSF are the foreign ordinary proxies; OTC liquidity will cap the move but the direction is right.

2. Compound Semiconductor Substrates and Foundries
TSEM, GFS: TSEM is catching the foundry tailwind from elevated compound semi demand. GFS is the laggard at -0.54%, reflecting its heavier exposure to legacy end markets versus the AI photonics buildout.
IQE, SOI: Both are the substrate picks-and-shovels plays. IQE's +2.87% is solid for an OTC foreign ordinary. SOI is quiet but holding green. These trade on InP and GaAs demand signals; AXTI's implosion is not read-through risk here since the B. Riley warning was AXTI-specific.
AXTI: Full breakdown. -17.27% on top of -16% yesterday. B. Riley's InP supply chain red flag plus Sumitomo locking a multi-year competing deal is a structural overhang, not a one-day blip. This name needs a clean catalyst to stabilize. Watch Chinese export permit news as the key re-entry variable.

3. Early-Stage Photonic Materials and PICs
SIVE: +4.11% is the standout here. Pre-revenue PIC name catching the optical super-cycle momentum. Low float amplifies the move.
POET, LWLG, ALMU: All three are red. Classic bifurcation: risk-off capital rotating into the higher-conviction large-cap names on a catalyst day. POET is down -5.72%, which is sharp for a session this green across the sector. Dilution overhang and pre-revenue status are limiting factors when the big dogs like LITE are running hard.

4. Semiconductor Equipment, Test, and Infrastructure
AEHR, FORM: Both posting solid gains. AEHR +6.80% is the standout in this bucket. Wafer-level burn-in demand tied to compound semi substrate volumes is the direct driver. FORM +4.04% is benefiting from the same underlying equipment cycle.
KEYS, GLW: KEYS is solid at +2.65%, moving on test and measurement read-through from AI infrastructure capex. GLW's +4.71% is a statement move for a large-cap fiber infrastructure name; hyperscaler optical buildout is pulling through on every link in the chain."}}}, "previous_counts": {"bookmark_count": 0, "favorite_count": 0, "quote_count": 0, "reply_count": 0, "retweet_count": 0}, "quotedRefResult": {"result": {"__typename": "Tweet", "rest_id": "2039080311888461995"}}, "rest_id": "2039438555513237796", "source": "<a href="https://mobile.twitter.com" rel="nofollow">Twitter Web App</a>", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "17147", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2039801775721763155", "source": "<a href="https://mobile.twitter.com" rel="nofollow">Twitter Web App</a>", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "37821", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

After verifying that some of Besi SMTA 2026 presentation slides have been already publicly available online, I decided to unpack it. Let's dive into the future of AI Chiplet Packaging, Hybrid Bonding, and CPO.

Along with the rapid growth of AI-related semiconductors, which are seeing a CAGR of 16.5% , the transition to 2.5D/3D systems that combine multiple chiplets is accelerating to overcome the limitations of traditional monolithic structures. To support this shift, Besi has partnered with Applied Materials to establish an integrated Die-to-Wafer (D2W) Hybrid Bonding line.

1. The Ultimate AI Package Architecture
The sheer complexity of next-gen AI architectures is staggering. It’s not just about stacking XPU and HBM anymore; we are seeing the integration of CPO (Co-Packaged Optics) with EIC-PIC Hybrid Bonding and high-precision photonics directly on the same substrate. System-level integration is reaching a whole new level.

2. The Equipment Roadmap
Besi’s equipment roadmap serves as a strong leading indicator for the industry. Both their advanced Hybrid Bonding systems (8800 CHAMEO) and ultra-precise Photonics placement tools (2200 evo 1µm) are targeting 2026 H1. The mass-production cycle for sub-10µm optical and AI packaging is shifting into high gear right now.

3. The "Why" Behind Hybrid Bonding
Why the aggressive push for Hybrid Bonding? The numbers speak for themselves. Compared to a 55µm pitch Si Bridge, moving to a 9µm pitch Hybrid Bonding approach delivers massive gains:
10X Lower Power 
10X Lower Added Latency 
30X Interconnect Area Reduction 
For power-hungry AI and high-speed optics, Cu-Cu direct bonding isn't just an option—it is mandatory.

4. The Scaling Battlefield
TCB (Thermo-compression bonding) is being pushed to its absolute limits, moving towards the 2µm pitch mark. Meanwhile, D2W (Die-to-Wafer) Hybrid Bonding is breaking through into sub-micron territory, driving an exponential increase in 3D interconnect density.
As interconnects scale below 10µm, the synergy between front-end wafer prep and back-end assembly is becoming the ultimate bottleneck."}}}, "rest_id": "2028011767138549891", "source": "<a href="https://mobile.twitter.com" rel="nofollow">Twitter Web App</a>", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "58940", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2039766774611423477", "source": "<a href="https://mobile.twitter.com" rel="nofollow">Twitter Web App</a>", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "11087", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

🔬 $GLW isn't just Gorilla Glass:

Corning invented optical fiber. They still dominate the market.

Every photonic connection starts with fiber. $GLW is the foundation layer. #photonics

Photonics in Southampton, semis in Wales, Manchester’s Ferranti heritage, Donald Michie’s pioneering ML group in Edinburgh, Belfast’s cyber frontier, and of course, DeepMind transforming the world from King’s Cross.

In this wave of UK AI, opportunity scaled is opportunity spread.

text:
Silicon Photonics Has Finally Entered the True 300 mm Era—But the Bottleneck Is No Longer Just the Device
If one sentence were to summarize the significance of OFC 2026 for silicon photonics manufacturing, it would be this: silicon photonics has finally moved from “can be built” to “can be mass-produced,” but the real point of competition is no longer at the level of individual devices—it lies in whether the entire ecosystem of platforms, testing, PDKs, packaging, and design tools can mature in sync.

Two of the most representative examples are STMicroelectronics’ PIC100 platform and Samsung Foundry’s 300 mm silicon photonics platform.
